questions.text: What should you do FIRST if you suspect your dog is choking?
options: Perform a blind finger sweep immediately
Restrain the dog gently and look inside the mouth
Give the dog water to dislodge the object
Leave the dog alone to cough it out
questions.explanation: Restraining the dog calmly and looking in the mouth first allows you to assess whether the object is visible and retrievable without risking pushing it deeper.

questions.text: What is the danger of performing a 'blind finger sweep' in a choking dog?
options: It can cause the dog to bark loudly
It may push the object deeper into the airway
It is too slow to be effective
It is only dangerous in cats, not dogs
questions.explanation: A blind finger sweep without being able to see the object risks pushing it further down the airway, making the blockage worse and more dangerous.

questions.text: Where should you apply Heimlich-style thrusts on a choking dog?
options: On the dog's head
On the sides of the rib cage
On the dog's lower back
Under the front legs
questions.explanation: Placing both hands on the side of the rib cage and applying firm, quick inward and upward pressure mimics the Heimlich manoeuvre and can dislodge the airway obstruction.

questions.text: After successfully removing the choking object, what should you do?
options: Give the dog a treat to calm it down
Nothing — the emergency is over
Still transport the dog to a veterinarian
Give the dog plenty of water
questions.explanation: Even after removing the object, internal damage may have occurred. A veterinarian should examine the dog to rule out airway injury, swelling, or aspiration.

questions.text: What should you do if the dog loses consciousness during a choking episode?
options: Begin rescue breathing into the dog's nose
Perform chest compressions only
Wait and see if the dog recovers on its own
Shake the dog vigorously to wake it
questions.explanation: If the dog is unconscious, rescue breathing — closing the mouth and breathing into the nose until the chest rises — can help maintain oxygenation while you arrange emergency veterinary transport.

questions.text: What should you NOT do if your dog has been poisoned?
options: Call the veterinarian immediately
Identify the suspected poison
Induce vomiting without vet guidance
Keep the dog calm and still
questions.explanation: Inducing vomiting without veterinary instruction is dangerous — some toxins cause greater damage when they come back up, and some require specific treatments rather than emesis.

questions.text: Why is it important to collect the suspected poison container?
options: So you can try to find an antidote yourself
To help the vet identify the exact toxin and treatment
So you can return it to the shop
It is not important
questions.explanation: Providing the exact substance, concentration, and quantity ingested allows the veterinarian to administer the correct antidote or treatment protocol promptly.

questions.text: If your dog is unconscious due to poisoning, how should you position it?
options: On its back with legs elevated
Sitting upright
On its side to prevent choking
In any comfortable position
questions.explanation: Positioning an unconscious dog on its side (recovery position) prevents the dog from choking on vomit and keeps the airway clear during transport.

questions.text: Which of the following is a common sign of dog poisoning?
options: Increased appetite
Excessive energy
Tremors or seizures
Bright pink gums
questions.explanation: Tremors, seizures, vomiting, drooling, dilated pupils, and disorientation are common signs of poisoning in dogs and require immediate veterinary attention.

questions.text: What should you give a poisoned dog while waiting for veterinary care?
options: Milk to coat the stomach
Nothing — no food or water unless instructed by a vet
Salt water to induce vomiting
Activated charcoal from your kitchen
questions.explanation: Unless specifically instructed by a veterinarian, you should give nothing by mouth. Milk, salt water, and home remedies can worsen the situation or interfere with medical treatment.

questions.text: Why should you wrap a cat in a towel before examining its mouth?
options: To keep the cat warm
To prevent the cat from scratching you
To help the cat breathe better
Towels are not useful in this situation
questions.explanation: A distressed cat in an emergency may scratch or bite even if normally gentle. Wrapping in a thick towel protects you from injury while allowing you to safely open the cat's mouth.

questions.text: How many abdominal thrusts should you apply to a choking cat before checking the mouth?
questions.explanation: Five gentle abdominal thrusts applied with two fingers against the cat's back are recommended before rechecking the mouth for the object, to balance effectiveness with the cat's fragility.

questions.text: Why is a full Heimlich manoeuvre NOT recommended for cats?
options: Cats are too fast to restrain
Cats have different lungs
Cats are too small and fragile — it may cause internal injury
The Heimlich manoeuvre only works on humans
questions.explanation: The full force used in a human or large dog Heimlich can rupture internal organs in a small cat. A gentler, modified version using two fingers is the safer approach.

questions.text: What should you do after helping a choking cat?
options: Feed the cat to check its swallowing is normal
Take the cat to a veterinarian immediately
Let the cat rest for a week before seeing a vet
Only see a vet if the cat stops eating
questions.explanation: Even if the object is removed, there may be airway irritation, swelling, or aspiration. Immediate veterinary assessment is always recommended after any choking episode.

questions.text: What is rescue breathing for a cat?
options: Pressing the chest repeatedly
Breathing into the cat's mouth only
Closing the cat's mouth and breathing into its nose
Laying the cat flat and waiting
questions.explanation: For cats (and dogs), rescue breathing involves closing the animal's mouth and gently breathing into the nose — this delivers air into the lungs when the animal cannot breathe independently.

questions.text: Which part of a rabbit's body is the most effective area to cool during heatstroke?
options: The tail
The ears
The feet
The belly
questions.explanation: Rabbits regulate body temperature primarily through their ears, which are rich in blood vessels. Gently wetting the ears with cool water is the most effective first cooling measure.

questions.text: What type of water should you use to cool a rabbit with heatstroke?
options: Ice water for fastest effect
Boiling water to open pores
Cool (not ice cold) water
Warm water
questions.explanation: Ice cold water causes shock in small animals like rabbits. Cool water reduces temperature gradually and safely without the risk of thermal shock.

questions.text: Why is rabbit heatstroke considered more dangerous than in dogs or cats?
options: Rabbits have thicker fur
Rabbits can die within minutes — they have very little heat tolerance
Rabbits cannot drink water
Rabbits overheat less often
questions.explanation: Rabbits are extremely vulnerable to heat and can suffer fatal organ failure within minutes of heatstroke onset. Immediate action and urgent veterinary care are critical.

questions.text: Should you take a rabbit to the vet if it appears to recover from heatstroke?
options: No — if it is moving normally, it is fine
Only if symptoms return after 24 hours
Yes — internal organ damage may not be immediately visible
No — just keep it cool for the rest of the day
questions.explanation: Rabbits mask signs of illness very well. Even if a rabbit appears to recover, internal kidney or liver damage from heatstroke can be life-threatening if not treated promptly by a vet.

questions.text: What should you NOT do when treating a rabbit with heatstroke?
options: Move it to a cool area
Wet its ears with cool water
Submerge the rabbit fully in water
Offer fresh cool water
questions.explanation: Fully submerging a rabbit in water causes extreme stress and can result in shock or drowning. Targeted cooling of the ears and gentle misting of the body is the correct approach.

questions.text: What should you do FIRST when you see your hamster having a seizure?
options: Pick it up immediately to comfort it
Stay calm and clear the area around the hamster
Put something in its mouth to prevent biting
Shake the hamster to wake it up
questions.explanation: The priority during a hamster seizure is to prevent it from injuring itself on cage objects. Stay calm, do not pick it up, and clear the area of anything that could cause harm.

questions.text: How long should a hamster seizure last before you consider it a serious emergency?
options: More than 30 seconds
More than 2 minutes
More than 10 minutes
Any seizure is immediately life-threatening
questions.explanation: A seizure lasting more than 2 minutes or seizures that recur require immediate veterinary attention, as prolonged seizures can cause brain damage or indicate a serious underlying condition.

questions.text: Why should you NOT put an object in a seizing hamster's mouth?
options: The hamster will chew and destroy it
It can break the hamster's teeth or cause injury
Hamsters do not have teeth
It helps but is just unnecessary
questions.explanation: Hamsters do not swallow their tongues during seizures. Inserting objects risks breaking teeth, injuring the mouth, or getting bitten during involuntary jaw movements.

questions.text: What environment should you create for a hamster recovering from a seizure?
options: Bright lights and loud music to stimulate it
A warm, quiet, dim environment
A cool, wet environment
Leave it in its normal cage with no changes
questions.explanation: After a seizure, the hamster is confused and sensitive. A warm, quiet, dimly lit space reduces sensory stimulation and stress, allowing the hamster to recover more comfortably.

questions.text: What information should you record to help your vet diagnose the cause of the seizure?
options: The hamster's favourite food
The colour of the hamster's eyes during the seizure
The duration and frequency of seizures
The time of the last cage cleaning
questions.explanation: Duration and frequency of seizures are critical diagnostic data for a veterinarian. They help differentiate between epilepsy, poisoning, infections, tumours, and other causes.
